answer.rationale: Lexical pattern is unambiguous. The Gethsemane prayer (Matt 26:39 ouch hos ego thelo G2309 all' hos sy, 'not as I will but as you will'; Luke 22:42 me to thelema mou alla to son ginestho G2307 + G1096, 'not my will but yours be done') presupposes two real wills in Christ, since a single divine will cannot oppose itself or pray for an alternative. John 6:38 lexically distinguishes to thelema to emon (my own will) from to thelema tou pempsantos me (the will of him who sent me). John 5:30 repeats the distinction. John 4:34 makes doing the Father's will Christ's food. Heb 10:7 quotes Ps 40:8 LXX with heko G2240 + tou poiesai to thelema sou G2307: Christ enters the world to do God's will. Heb 5:7-8 names 'the days of his flesh' (sarkos G4561) with prayers and supplications and learned obedience, the human will operating. The Father's will and Christ's praying-yielding will are two referents in the same scene.
answer.notes: Two-wills (dyothelitism) is the Christological corollary of two natures (Chalcedon, 451). The Third Council of Constantinople in 681 settled it as the sixth ecumenical council: Christ has two natural wills and two natural operations, the human will following and submitting to the divine will, not opposed to it. Public-record carriers of denial: monothelitism (one will, the divine, replacing or absorbing the human) arose in 7th-century Byzantium as an imperial compromise to reconcile Chalcedonians with miaphysite churches; condemned at Constantinople III. Named carriers condemned at the council were Sergius, Pyrrhus, Paul, Theodore of Pharan, Cyrus, Macarius, and Pope Honorius. The historic Maronite Church held monothelite teaching in some early-medieval periods but has since entered full communion with Rome and formally renounced monothelitism. Some popular evangelical preaching subtly drifts toward functional monothelitism when it presents Christ's obedience as the divine will alone, leaving no real human will to submit. Cult-marker calibration: Maximus the Confessor (c. 580-662) is the chief patristic defender of dyothelitism. He refused to sign the Typos of Constans II, had his tongue uprooted and his right hand cut off, and died in exile; he is vindicated by the Sixth Ecumenical Council and venerated as a saint in Eastern Orthodoxy and Catholicism. would_die_for is true because the doctrine guards the reality of Christ's full humanity (which Maximus understood: 'what is not assumed is not healed') and the load-bearing logic of the atonement: a human will had to obey where Adam's human will disobeyed. cult_marker_if_denied is false honestly. Monothelitism is heterodox by ecumenical conscience but is conventionally classified heterodox-not-cult, in the same general bracket as miaphysitism or Nestorianism: a fundamental Christological mis-step that does not constitute a different gospel the way Mormon, Jehovah's Witness, or Iglesia ni Cristo Christology does. The 1 John 4:2-3 antichrist test names denial of Christ come in the flesh, not denial of two wills specifically. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: most lay Christians across all lineages cannot articulate dyothelitism by name but functionally confess it when they pray the Lord's Prayer ('thy will be done') as Christ taught and prayed it in Gethsemane. Lineages that confess two natures and Chalcedonian Christology functionally entail two wills even where they do not name the doctrine. Engagement ladder: would not serve or let children be taught a monothelite church because the error undermines the meaningful humanity of Christ's obedience, but would visit, participate, marry, and publicly correct where appropriate.

stem_audit.notes: Statement 'Christ possesses two wills (divine and human) corresponding to His two natures, working in harmony' restates the lexical force of Matt 26:39, Luke 22:42, John 6:38, John 5:30, and Heb 10:7. The 'two natures' clause is Chalcedonian vocabulary, but the parallel 'two wills' clause is grounded directly in the Gethsemane lexical pattern (theology of two referents, my will vs your will) and the Johannine programmatic statements where Christ distinguishes to thelema to emon from to thelema tou pempsantos me. 'Working in harmony' is the Constantinople III qualifier (the human will follows and submits to the divine, not opposed or contrary), itself a paraphrase of Luke 22:42 ginestho. No confessional smuggling beyond the lexical evidence.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: Jesus Christ has two wills, one divine and one human, that always work together in harmony. The human will always freely follows the divine will, never against it. The strongest reason from Scripture is the Gethsemane prayer. In Luke 22:42 and Matthew 26:39 Jesus prays 'Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me. Yet not my will, but yours, be done.' Jesus names two distinct wills in the same sentence. He distinguishes his will from the Father's will, then yields his will to the Father's will. If Jesus had only one will (the divine), the prayer makes no sense, because a single divine will cannot pray against itself or ask itself for a different outcome. John 6:38 puts it even more directly: 'I have come down from heaven not to do my own will, but the will of him who sent me.' John 5:30 says the same. John 4:34 says doing the Father's will is Jesus' food. Hebrews 10:7 puts Psalm 40 on Jesus' lips: 'I have come to do your will, O God.' Hebrews 5:7 and 8 say that in the days of his flesh he offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears, and learned obedience through what he suffered. Real human obedience requires a real human will that can choose to obey. The strongest counterargument is that Jesus is one person, not two, and some early teachers worried that two wills would split Christ into two persons. They suggested that maybe Christ has only one will, the divine, and the human nature simply goes along. This is called monothelitism. The verdict handles this by noting that wills belong to natures, not persons. Christ is one person, but he has two complete natures, and each nature has its own faculty of willing. The human will is not absorbed or overruled. It freely chooses to do what the divine will commands, just as Jesus showed in Gethsemane. The two wills cooperate perfectly, never in conflict, because the human will is sinless and always yields. This also matters for salvation. An ancient saying captures it: what is not assumed is not healed. If Christ had no human will, then no human will was ever brought into obedient submission to God in his earthly life, and the human will is not saved. The point of the cross is that a real human said yes to God where the first human said no.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: The two-wills doctrine (sometimes called dyothelitism) is confessed by every major historic Christian lineage that follows the ecumenical councils. The Third Council of Constantinople in 681, recognised as the Sixth Ecumenical Council, settled the question. It declared two natural wills and two natural operations in Christ, with the human will following and submitting to the divine will, not opposed to it. The council condemned monothelitism (the one-will view) and named Sergius, Pyrrhus, Paul, Theodore of Pharan, Cyrus, Macarius, and Pope Honorius among those teaching it. The Catholic Church confesses two wills and two operations in Christ. The Catechism teaches that Christ's human will does not resist or oppose his divine will but is subject to it and follows it. Catholics venerate Maximus the Confessor, the chief patristic defender of two wills, who suffered mutilation and exile for refusing to sign an imperial decree forbidding the doctrine. Eastern Orthodoxy holds the same teaching with particular reverence for Maximus, who is honoured as a great Father and Confessor. Lutherans, Anglicans, Reformed, Methodists, Anabaptists, and most Pentecostals confess two natures in one person following Chalcedon, which carries dyothelitism by implication; their confessional documents do not always name two wills explicitly but they do not deny it either. The Augsburg Confession Article III, the 39 Articles Article II, the Westminster Confession 8.2, the Methodist Articles of Religion Article II, and the Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths all confess two complete natures in one person, which presupposes the faculties of will proper to each nature. Public-record carriers of denial. Monothelitism is the 7th-century heresy that Christ has only one will, the divine, with the human will absorbed or replaced. It originated as a Byzantine imperial compromise to reconcile Chalcedonian and miaphysite Christians and was condemned at Constantinople in 681. The historic Maronite Church in Lebanon held some monothelite teaching in earlier centuries but has since formally renounced it and entered full communion with Rome. There is no continuing major Christian denomination that openly confesses monothelitism today. Some popular evangelical preaching subtly drifts toward functional monothelitism when it presents Christ's obedience as nothing but the divine will at work, with no real human will to submit, but this is a teaching error to correct, not a settled position. Monothelitism is classified as heterodox by historic Christianity but is typically not classified at the cult-grade level. It is in the same general bracket as miaphysitism or Nestorianism: a serious Christological mis-step but not a different gospel in the sense that Mormon, Jehovah's Witness, or Iglesia ni Cristo Christology constitutes a different gospel.

scripture.key_term: μὴ τὸ θέλημά μου ἀλλὰ τὸ σὸν γινέσθω (me to thelema mou alla to son ginestho) G2307 + G1096
scripture.force: thelema G2307 = will, act of volition, determination. The construction names two distinct referents: to thelema mou (my will) versus to son (yours), connected by the contrastive alla (but). ginestho G1096 = let it be / let it come to pass (present middle/passive imperative 3rd singular of ginomai). Christ in Gethsemane names his own will, names the Father's will, and yields the first to the second. A single divine will cannot pray against itself; the contrast presupposes two real wills in one person

scripture.key_term: πλὴν οὐχ ὡς ἐγὼ θέλω ἀλλ' ὡς σύ (plen ouch hos ego thelo all' hos sy) G2309
scripture.force: thelo G2309 = to will, wish, intend (1st person singular present indicative active). The construction parallels Luke's: 'nevertheless, not as I will but as you'. The implicit second thelo for sy is suppressed. ego thelo and sy (theleis) are two distinct subjects of willing. plen = nevertheless, but, however, marking the decisive turn from request to yielding

scripture.key_term: ἀλλ' οὐ τί ἐγὼ θέλω ἀλλὰ τί σύ (all' ou ti ego thelo alla ti sy) G2309
scripture.force: Mark preserves the Aramaic abba (G5) before paraphrasing into Greek. Same lexical pattern as Matthew and Luke: 'not what I will, but what you'. The threefold synoptic witness establishes the Gethsemane formula. Three witnesses, one prayer, two named wills

scripture.key_term: οὐχ ἵνα ποιῶ τὸ θέλημα τὸ ἐμὸν ἀλλὰ τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με (to thelema to emon... to thelema tou pempsantos me) G2307 + G3992
scripture.force: thelema G2307 appears twice in one sentence with two distinct possessors: to emon (mine, my own) and tou pempsantos me (of him who sent me). pempsantos G3992 = having sent (aorist active participle, of pempo). Christ in the Bread of Life discourse explicitly distinguishes his own will from the Father's will and identifies his incarnational mission as doing the latter not the former. The grammatical possessive cleanly separates two referents

scripture.key_term: οὐ ζητῶ τὸ θέλημα τὸ ἐμὸν ἀλλὰ τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με (ou zeto to thelema to emon) G2212 + G2307
scripture.force: zeto G2212 = I seek (present indicative active 1st singular). Christ says of himself 'I do not seek my own will but the will of him who sent me'. The grammatical structure is identical to John 6:38. Christ has a will (to emon, my own) and he chooses not to seek it but the Father's. The very act of 'not seeking' one's will requires having one to refuse

scripture.key_term: ἐμὸν βρῶμά ἐστιν ἵνα ποιήσω τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με (emon broma... thelema tou pempsantos me) G1033 + G2307 + G5048
scripture.force: broma G1033 = food. thelema G2307 = will. teleioso G5048 = to bring to completion, finish (aorist subjunctive active). Christ to the disciples at the well: 'My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to finish his work.' The Father's will is the object of Christ's mission, distinct from his own desire for food

scripture.key_term: ἥκω... τοῦ ποιῆσαι ὁ θεὸς τὸ θέλημά σου (heko... to thelema sou) G2240 + G2307
scripture.force: heko G2240 = I have come, am present (present indicative active 1st singular). thelema sou = your will (the Father's). Quoting Psalm 40:8 (LXX 39:9), Hebrews puts the words on Christ's lips as he enters the world: 'Behold, I have come to do your will, O God.' The will being done is the Father's, freely embraced by the incarnate Son. The whole logic of Heb 10 turns on this voluntary doing of God's will as the once-for-all sacrifice

scripture.key_term: ἰδοὺ ἥκω τοῦ ποιῆσαι τὸ θέλημά σου (idou heko tou poiesai to thelema sou) G2240 + G4160 + G2307
scripture.force: Hebrews 10:9 repeats the formula and draws the inference: 'He takes away the first to establish the second.' The doing of God's will by the incarnate Christ is the basis on which the old sacrificial order is set aside and the new covenant established. v.10 then says 'by that will we have been sanctified.' Christ's human-willed obedience and the Father's will converge in the atonement

scripture.key_term: ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις τῆς σαρκὸς αὐτοῦ... δεήσεις τε καὶ ἱκετηρίας... μετὰ κραυγῆς ἰσχυρᾶς καὶ δακρύων (sarkos autou... deeseis kai hiketerias) G4561 + G1162 + G2428
scripture.force: sarx G4561 = flesh. deesis G1162 = supplication. hiketeria G2428 = entreaty, supplication (a hapax in the NT). 'In the days of his flesh, having offered up both prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears.' Real human anguish, real human pleading. A being with only the divine will does not plead with himself in supplication and tears. The verse names Christ's human-side activity

scripture.key_term: ἔμαθεν ἀφ' ὧν ἔπαθεν τὴν ὑπακοήν (emathen aph' hon epathen ten hypakoen) G3129 + G3958 + G5218
scripture.force: manthano G3129 = to learn (aorist active indicative). pascho G3958 = to suffer. hypakoe G5218 = obedience. 'Though he was a Son, he learned obedience from what he suffered.' Learning and obeying are activities of a human faculty. The divine will, omniscient and immutable, does not learn obedience. Christ's human will learned obedience in the school of suffering. This is dyothelitism stated lexically

scripture.key_term: ἡ ψυχή μου τετάρακται (he psyche mou tetaraktai) G5590 + G5015
scripture.force: psyche G5590 = soul. tarasso G5015 = to trouble, agitate (perfect passive indicative). 'Now is my soul troubled. And what shall I say? Father, save me from this hour. But for this purpose I have come to this hour.' Christ names a troubled human soul, considers a prayer to escape, then commits to the Father's purpose. The internal dialogue between human-side dread and divine-purpose commitment is the dyothelitic structure

scripture.key_term: γενηθήτω τὸ θέλημά σου (genetheto to thelema sou) G1096 + G2307
scripture.force: Christ's second prayer in Gethsemane drops the request and intensifies the yielding: 'If this cannot pass unless I drink it, your will be done.' The same phrase Christ taught the disciples to pray in Matt 6:10 (genetheto to thelema sou) is now prayed by Christ himself, with his own will yielding to the Father's. The Lord's Prayer presupposes a will distinct from God's that must align with God's; Christ models it

scripture.key_term: προέκοπτεν σοφίᾳ καὶ ἡλικίᾳ (proekopten sophia kai helikia) G4298
scripture.force: prokopto G4298 = to advance, progress (imperfect indicative active, continuous past). Jesus kept advancing in wisdom and stature. The imperfect rules out a single moment of pretended growth. Real growth in wisdom requires a real human mind and a real human faculty of willed attention. This grounds the rational human soul that Apollinarianism denied and the human will that monothelitism denied

scripture.key_term: לַעֲשׂוֹת רְצוֹנְךָ אֱלֹהַי חָפָצְתִּי (laasot retsoncha... chafatzti) H7522 + H2654
scripture.force: ratzon H7522 = pleasure, will, favour. chafetz H2654 = to delight in, take pleasure in (Qal perfect 1st singular). 'To do your will, my God, I delight.' The MT Hebrew of Ps 40:8 (40:7 in English) supplies the substrate Hebrews 10:7 quotes via the LXX (Ps 39:9). The incarnate Christ takes these words as his own programmatic statement of voluntary obedience. The Hebrew names a willing delight, the Greek names a coming to do; both presuppose a doer with a will of his own who freely aligns with God

scripture.key_term: περὶ δὲ τῆς ἡμέρας ἐκείνης... οὐδὲ ὁ υἱός εἰ μὴ ὁ πατήρ (oude ho huios) G3761
scripture.force: Concerning that day and hour, no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father. The Son's not-knowing is a genuine limitation predicable of him only on the human side. A single divine will is omniscient and cannot not-know. The verse complicates one-will Christology and supports two real centres of knowing and willing in one person

scripture.key_term: ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν (ego kai ho pater hen esmen)
scripture.force: 'I and the Father are one.' hen (neuter, not heis masculine) = one thing, one in essence, not one person. The verse asserts ontological oneness of the Son and Father, sometimes used to imply one will. The neuter form, however, names unity of nature and operation, not numerical unity of person or will-faculty. Read with John 6:38 (two wills named), the verse is harmonised by the Chalcedonian-Constantinople III distinction: one in nature, two faculties of will corresponding to two natures

scripture.key_term: ὑπήκοος μέχρι θανάτου (hypekoos mechri thanatou) G5255
scripture.force: hypekoos G5255 = obedient (adjective). 'He humbled himself, becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' Obedience is an activity of a will that submits to another will. The divine will of God the Son is not obedient to the Father in his eternal procession; obedience belongs to the incarnate kenotic state in which the human will yields. Phil 2 names this obedience and locates it in his having taken the form of a servant

scripture.key_term: γενηθήτω τὸ θέλημά σου (genetheto to thelema sou) G1096 + G2307
scripture.force: 'Your will be done on earth as it is in heaven.' Christ teaches the disciples to pray for God's will to be done by creaturely wills that are distinct from God's. He himself prays this verbatim in Gethsemane (Matt 26:42). The Lord's Prayer presupposes the dyothelitic structure: creaturely wills exist and are called to align freely with God's. Christ models the alignment from his human side

hermeneutics.notes: The verdict rests on the grammatical force of the Gethsemane prayer (Matt 26:39 / Mark 14:36 / Luke 22:42) and the Johannine programmatic statements (John 4:34, 5:30, 6:38), all of which lexically distinguish Christ's will from the Father's will using thelema G2307 / thelo G2309. Hebrews 10:7-10 and 5:7-8 reinforce by placing Christ in the role of one who comes to do God's will, who prays with loud cries and tears, and who learns obedience. Analogia scripturae is invoked to harmonise John 10:30 ('I and the Father are one') with John 6:38 (two wills): the Father-Son unity is ontological (one in nature, hen neuter) and operational (cooperating perfectly), not a denial of two faculties of will corresponding to two natures. The doctrine does not require any non-default framework. All Chalcedonian-confessing lineages (Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Lutheran, Anglican, Reformed, Methodist, Anabaptist, most Pentecostals) hold dyothelitism either explicitly or by implication from two natures. Progressive revelation is not the operative factor; the doctrine is fully resident in the Gospels and the Pauline-Hebrews corpus.

counter_witness.anchor: Third Council of Constantinople, Sixth Ecumenical Council, Dogmatic Definition (AD 681)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Two natural wills and two natural operations in him, indivisibly, inconvertibly, inseparably, inconfusedly. His human will following, not resisting, but rather subject to his divine and almighty will

counter_witness.anchor: Maximus the Confessor (c. 580-662), defender of dyothelitism against the Typos of Constans II
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ possessed two natural wills (divine and human) corresponding to his two natures. Maximus refused to sign the imperial monothelite decree, had his tongue uprooted and right hand cut off, died in exile, was vindicated at Constantinople III

One and the same Christ in two natures without confusion, change, division, separation, the distinction of natures being preserved. Dyothelitism is the operational corollary: two natures entail two faculties of will

Christ possesses two wills and two natural operations, divine and human. They are not opposed to each other, but cooperate. The human will of his Son does not resist his divine will but is subject to it. Citing Constantinople III

counter_witness.anchor: Venerable Maximus the Confessor, commemorated January 21 in Eastern Orthodox liturgy
counter_witness.key_phrase: Two natural wills in Christ, one divine and one human. The Orthodox Church venerates Maximus as Confessor and Father, who suffered tongue and hand mutilation for refusing to deny the two wills of Christ

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article II, Of the Word or Son of God, which was made very Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: Two whole and perfect Natures, that is to say, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one Person, never to be divided. Two natures entail two faculties of will by Chalcedonian implication

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article III, Of the Son of God (1530)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Two natures, the divine and the human, inseparably enjoined in one Person, true God and true man. The Formula of Concord Solid Declaration VIII preserves the distinct properties of each nature including the operations of will

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion, Article II (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles, 1784)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Two whole and perfect natures, that is to say, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one person, never to be divided. Carries dyothelitism by Chalcedonian implication

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith 8.2, Of Christ the Mediator
counter_witness.key_phrase: Two whole, perfect, and distinct natures, the Godhead and the manhood, were inseparably joined together in one person, without conversion, composition, or confusion. Each nature with its proper faculties of will and operation

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, The Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Lord Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God, virgin-born, sinless human life. Standard Chalcedonian Christology presupposing two natures and (by implication) two wills
Christ was conceived by the Holy Ghost in the Virgin Mary, and was born of her, and thus became flesh and man. Confesses Chalcedonian two natures, dyothelitism by implication, not explicitly named

web.quote: Luke 22:42: me to thelema mou alla to son ginestho (G2307 + G1096). Two distinct wills named in one prayer, with Christ yielding his own to the Father's
Matt 26:39: ouch hos ego thelo all' hos sy (G2309). 'Not as I will but as you'. ego thelo and the implicit sy theleis are two distinct subjects of willing
John 6:38: to thelema to emon vs to thelema tou pempsantos me. Two occurrences of thelema G2307 in one verse with two distinct possessors. Lexically unambiguous
John 5:30: ou zeto to thelema to emon alla to thelema tou pempsantos me. 'I do not seek my own will but the will of him who sent me.' Distinguishing my-will from the-Father's-will
Heb 10:7: heko... to thelema sou (G2240 + G2307). Quoting Ps 40:8 LXX. Christ enters the world saying 'I have come to do your will, O God'
Heb 5:7: en tais hemerais tes sarkos autou... deeseis te kai hiketerias meta krauges ischyras kai dakryon. 'In the days of his flesh, prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears'
Ps 40:8 MT: laasot retsoncha elohai chafatzti (H7522 + H2654). 'To do your will my God I delight.' The substrate Heb 10:7 quotes via LXX. Voluntary willed delight in God's will

Constantinople III (AD 681): two natural volitions or wills in him and two natural principles of action. His human will following, not resisting, but in fact subject to his divine and all-powerful will
Maximus championed that Jesus Christ possessed two wills (one divine and one human). Refused the Typos, tongue uprooted, right hand cut off. Vindicated at Constantinople III as Father of the Church
39 Articles II: two whole and perfect Natures, that is to say, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one Person, never to be divided. Chalcedonian two natures entailing two wills
Methodist Articles II: two whole and perfect natures, that is to say, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one person, never to be divided. Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles

Monothelitism: 7th-century doctrine that Christ has only one will, originating as Byzantine compromise with miaphysite churches, condemned at Constantinople III (681). Historic Maronite Church has since renounced it
